title: ‘Overvalued’: Why the CBA rout may worsen
full_text:
Commonwealth Bank’s slight share price rebound offers cold comfort after the nation’s biggest bank suffered its worst one-day fall in history.
Its shares closed up 1.8 per cent to $156.42 after hitting a fresh three-month low of $151.
At this stage it looks to be more of a so-called “dead cat bounce”, or fleeting recovery, after a record 10.4 per cent drop on Wednesday wiped a massive $30bn off the value of the nation’s biggest bank following tax changes in the Albanese government’s budget.
In financial market lingo, a dead cat bounce is one that proves very limited and a harbinger of further declines. Consider that when CBA fell 10 per cent on March 16, 2020, in the early days of Covid-19, it bounced a decisive 13 per cent the next day, marking a major low.
During the global financial crisis on December 18, 2008, CBA fell 9 per cent and then bounced 4.9 per cent. And while it made a new low three weeks later, it was getting close to the bottom.
The concern is that even after the biggest one-day fall on record, analysts say CBA shares remain wildly overvalued. At the same time, changes to negative gearing and capital gains tax in the federal budget are likely to have a negative impact on property prices and investor loan growth.
Bloomberg’s consensus 12-month price target of $133.62 for CBA implies a 13 per cent fall that would wipe out the forecast dividend yield of 3.4 per cent, even after franking credits.
The consensus has been dragged higher to some extent because of the relentless rise in CBA’s share price since late 2023, which saw it double to a record $192 a share in late June.
At $90 to $142.26, the range of analyst 12-month target prices for CBA has a negative skew.
There are 13 sell ratings, three hold ratings and no buy ratings on the stock. The timing of Wednesday’s plunge was significant.
Matt Comyn-led CBA released its third-quarter trading update a day after the government unveiled sweeping changes to property tax concessions that target the investor loan market, where CBA dominates.
Morningstar analyst Nathan Zaia says CBA shares remain “materially overvalued”. “On a forward P/E of around 24 and a dividend yield of 3 per cent, valuation multiples are difficult to reconcile with a mid-single-digit earnings growth outlook,” Zaia said.
His fair value estimate sits at just $105 a share, implying the stock needs to fall another 32 per cent even after Wednesday’s bloodbath.
The budget changes strike at the heart of CBA’s recent success. Housing credit growth ran at a robust 7.3 per cent in the 12 months to March 2026, with investor loans surging 9.6 per cent.
Morningstar expects this to slow sharply to just 3 to 4 per cent in fiscal 2027 as the tax changes bite. “We expect changes to tax concessions for investors, mainly hitting existing properties, plus higher interest rates, which lower borrowing capacity, to slow housing credit growth,” Zaia says.
Macquarie analyst Victor German says with rising interest rates, a slowing economy and the tax changes, there is “downside risk to credit growth across the sector", and CBA’s valuation “remains difficult to justify".
Similarly, Morgan Stanley analyst Richard Wiles says “de-rating risk is rising” for CBA due to macro headwinds, weaker housing market sentiment and a tighter capital position.
“CBA still trades on a one-year forward P/E of about 23 times, well above the five-year pre-Covid average of about 14 times,” he said.
Goldman Sachs analyst Brendon Sproules has a sell rating on CBA after a “historic rally in its share price despite a flat earnings growth profile”.
Wealth Within senior analyst Filip Tortevski questions whether Wednesday’s fall is “merely a temporary setback or the definitive signal of a looming, long-term technical collapse”.
“What makes the current set-up so concerning is how closely it resembles those previous topping periods” before major corrections during the GFC and between 2015 and Covid, Tortevski warned.
“If history rhymes, a move back towards $95 cannot be ruled out, which would imply another potential 50 per cent decline from the highs.” For now, CBA shareholders face an uncomfortable reality. Their bank remains profitable and dominant but the valuation party appears over. As well, the budget changes threaten a key growth engine just as interest rates rise further.
summary: Commonwealth Bank's shares fell sharply after the government announced tax changes, leading analysts to believe the stock is overvalued and may drop further. Despite a small rebound, experts warn of ongoing risks to the bank's growth and valuation.
author: DAVID ROGERS EXCHANGE
heading: Banking and Economy
key_points: CBA shares dropped 10.4% in one day, losing $30 billion in value.
Analysts believe CBA shares are overvalued, with a 12-month price target of $133.62.
Changes to negative gearing and capital gains tax could slow property prices and investor loan growth.
CBA's recent success in housing credit growth may be threatened by these tax changes and rising interest rates.
Analysts have issued 13 sell ratings and no buy ratings for CBA.
ticker_line: CBA shares plunge amid tax change fears
